one_line_takeaway: By mapping the optimization sub-components of prior black-box jailbreak attacks/defenses onto dependency DAGs and greedily ensembling the best of each, AutoJailbreak yields attacks that hit up to 99% success (and +50 points on GPT-4) and a mixture-of-defenders that pins jailbreak rates to single digits across dynamic, static, and white-box attacks — arguing such ensembles should be the minimal robustness benchmark.
key_finding: Ensembling optimization sub-components chosen via dependency/causal analysis substantially beats the individual state-of-the-art methods it is built from. Ensemble Attack-GA reaches JR >90% on four models (91.7% GPT-3.5; 99% on Vicuna, Qwen, and Mistral) while keeping hallucination rate low; Ensemble Attack-Gen gives the largest gains on the hardest, best-aligned models, surpassing the best baseline on GPT-4 by 50 points and on Claude by 36.5 points. On defense, the mixture-of-defenders AutoDefense drives successful-jailbreak rate to single digits (often ~0-2%) across dynamic, static, and white-box GCG attacks where single-strategy defenses each only cover one attack class — e.g. against GCG on LLaMa-2 it cuts JR from 100% (no defense) to 0% while holding hallucination to 10.7% vs summarize's ~40%. A secondary but important finding: on the TAP attack, LLM-as-a-Judge (TPR 65.22%, FPR 8.22%) vastly outperforms a fine-tuned toxic classifier (TPR 8.70%) and keyword matching (TPR 34.78%, FPR 21.92%), justifying LLM-as-a-Judge; and separating 'hallucination' from genuine 'alignment' reveals that many apparent defense successes are actually off-topic non-answers rather than value-aligned refusals.
core_contribution: AutoJailbreak is a framework that analyzes existing black-box LLM jailbreak attacks and defenses through a 'dependency lens', using causal analysis and directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) to map how optimization sub-components of prior methods depend on and improve over one another. From this analysis the authors build three of their OWN artifacts: (1) AutoAttack, which produces two ensemble attacks — Ensemble Attack-GA (combining optimization tricks from AutoDAN-GA, GPTFuzzer, OpenSesame, and SMJ across a genetic-algorithm framework's seed-init/selection/mutation steps) and Ensemble Attack-Gen (combining Tastle, PAIR, TAP, and SBJ across an LLM-red-teaming adversarial-generation framework); (2) AutoDefense, a mixture-of-defenders (MoD) ensemble that routes prompts to a defense expert for adversarial suffixes (DE-adv: spell-check + Monte-Carlo suffix erasure) or malicious semantics (DE-sem: summarize + intention-analysis rewrite), followed by a post-generation voting-judge filter; and (3) AutoEvaluation, a two-stage LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation that separates a third outcome class — hallucination / off-topic responses — from genuine jailbreak success and genuine alignment (refusal). The authors argue AutoJailbreak should serve as a minimal robustness benchmark that any new attack or defense must be tested against.
threat_model: Query-based BLACK-BOX threat model reflecting API-based LLM usage. Attacker A can only interact with victim model M_V by submitting a jailbreak prompt P_J = P_I ⊕ T (an initial malicious behavior P_I such as 'How to make a bomb' combined via placeholder-replacement or appending with a malicious template/system prompt T) and observing the API response R. The attacker has NO access to model parameters, the next-token probability distribution, or sampling hyperparameters. On the defense side, defenders may only modify the incoming prompt (P_J -> P_J') or the output (R -> R'), also without access to model internals; a defense must generalize to BOTH malicious-semantics prompts and adversarial-suffix prompts. The paper explicitly EXCLUDES long-tail-encoding attacks (low-resource languages, ASCII art) and transfer-based attacks from evaluation for fairness. White-box GCG is used only as an external attack to stress-test AutoDefense, not as part of the attacker's own capability set.
limitations: The 'causal analysis' is qualitative/heuristic: the authors admit prior works rarely hold all-but-one factor constant, so their DAG edges are informed judgments rather than controlled causal estimates, and they simply pick the terminal node of each route without ablating alternatives. Attack scope is deliberately narrowed — long-tail encoding and transfer attacks are excluded, so 'break all tested models' does not cover those surfaces. Ensemble Attack-Gen does NOT always win (it trails the leading attack by 4.2 and 6.2 points on some models), and AutoDefense is sometimes slightly worse than a single baseline on Qwen/Mistral in the GA setting. Using GPT-4 as the attack model for LLaMa-2/-3/Claude (because they refuse to red-team) breaks the same-model-as-victim design used elsewhere, introducing an inconsistency. The evaluation dataset is small (96 behaviors) and the TPR/FPR evaluator comparison (Table 1) is measured only on the TAP attack. The whole pipeline depends heavily on auxiliary LLMs (language assistant for benign/malicious routing, similarity scorer, voting judge), so cost/latency and the reliability of those judges are not deeply analyzed. It is a preprint (under review) without released reproduction artifacts described in the text.

datasets_used: Authors' own 96 malicious-behavior dataset (14 general subcategories + 10 target subcategories, 4 behaviors each), derived from ChatGPT and Gemini usage-policy categories
quantitative_results: Ensemble Attack-GA JR: 91.7% GPT-3.5, 99.0% Vicuna, 99.0% Qwen, 99.0% Mistral, 65.6% GPT-4, 63.5% LLaMa-2, 40.0% LLaMa-3, 5.2% Claude
Ensemble Attack-Gen surpasses best baseline on GPT-4 by ~50 points (80.2% JR vs 30.2% for TAP) and on Claude by 36.5 points (39.6% vs 3.1%)
Ensemble Attack-Gen best on 5 of 8 models, trails leader by only 4.2 and 6.2 points on the others
Evaluator comparison on TAP (Table 1) TPR/FPR: fine-tuned classifier 8.70%/5.48%, keyword matching 34.78%/21.92%, LLM-as-a-Judge 65.22%/8.22%
AutoDefense vs GCG white-box on LLaMa-2 (Table 5): JR 100%->0% with HR 10.7% / AR 89.3%, vs summarize 0% JR but 39.3% HR and intention-analysis 39.3% JR
AutoDefense on Ensemble-Adv attack: GPT-4 JR 80.2%->0% (AR 99.0%), Qwen 45.8%->0% (AR 100%), Vicuna 21.9%->0%
AutoDefense on static malicious-template attacks: JR reduced to ~0.5-1.6% across all 8 models (e.g. Qwen 83.8%->0.5%, Mistral 84.8%->1.6%)
Roberta classifier fine-tuned on 1,000 sampled pairs per dataset, 5 epochs, batch size 16
baselines_compared: TAP / Tree of Attacks (adversarial-generation attack baseline)
Monte-Carlo sampling suffix erasure (defense baseline / DE-adv component)
GCG (white-box attack used to stress-test defenses)
